content.full_text:
REMCHO, JOHANSEN & PURCELL
ATTORNEYS AT LAW

220 MONTGOMERY STREET, SUITE 800
SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94104
415/398-6230    FAX:415/398-7256

May 9, 1991

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Santos Advice Letter, File No. I-90-650

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

     On behalf of the California Political Attorneys
Association, I write to request reconsideration of the Santos
Advice Letter, dated December 14, 1990. The letter
concludes:

     Under the provisions of the Act, when a
     mass mailing is sent at the behest of a
     candidate or candidates each candidate is
     deemed to be a sender and must be
     identified on the outside of a political
     mailing which consists of over 200
     substantially similar pieces of mail sent
     out in a calendar month.

     The only "authorities" cited are an earlier advice
letter (Bagatelos Advice Letter, No. I-88-383) and language
in the 1986 Information Manual on Campaign Disclosure
Provisions of the Political Reform Act. However, both
sources are squarely contradicted by the Commission's own
regulation 18435, defining the term "sender." There is no
mention of that regulation in the Santos letter, the
Bagatelos letter, or the Information Manual.¹ The regulation
provides as follows:

     (a) A "mass mailing" has been made when
     200 or more identical, or nearly

_______________
     ¹ Similar advice was given to Santos without analysis in
the Ferguson Advice Letter, No. A-86-044, and in the June,
1990 FPPC Bulletin. Neither was cited in the Santos letter.

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
May 9, 1991
Page 2


                    identical, pieces of mail have been sent
                    within a calendar month.

                    (b)  The sender, as used in Government
                    Code Section 84305⁽²⁾, is the candidate or
                    committee who pays for the largest
                    portion of expenditures attributable to
                    the designing, printing, and posting of


    ___________________

    ² Government Code section 84305 provides:

         (a)  Except as provided in subdivision
         (b), no candidate or committee shall send
         a mass mailing unless the name, street
         address, and city of the candidate or
         committee are shown on the outside of
         each piece of mail in the mass mailing
         and on at least one of the inserts
         included within each piece of mail of the
         mailing in no less than 6-point type
         which shall be in a color or print which
         contrasts with the background so as to be
         easily legible. A post office box may be
         stated in lieu of a street address if the
         organization's address is a matter of
         public record with the Secretary of
         State.

         (b)  If the sender of the mass mailing is
         a single candidate or committee, the
         name, street address, and city of the
         candidate or committee need only be shown
         on the outside of each piece of mail.

         (c)  If the sender of a mass mailing is a
         controlled committee, the name of the
         person controlling the committee shall be
         included in addition to the information
         required by subdivision (a).

                              (Gov. Code, § 84305.)

    The term "sender" is not defined in the Political Reform Act
    itself.

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
May 9, 1991
Page 3


the mailing which are reportable under
Government Code Sections 84200-84217.

(c) For purposes of this section to "pay
for" a share of the cost of a mass
mailing means to make, to promise to
make, or to incur an obligation to make,
any payment:

(1) To any person for the design,
printing, postage, materials or other
costs (including salaries, fees, or
commissions) of the mailing; or

(2) As a fee or other consideration
for an endorsement or, in the case of a
ballot measure, support or opposition, in
the mailing.

(2 Cal. Code of Regs., § 18435,
emphasis added.)

Regulation 18435 clearly establishes that a
candidate becomes the sender of a mass mailing only by paying
for the mailing. Nothing in the regulation supports the
Santos letter's conclusion that a candidate is "deemed" to be
the sender if the mailing is sent at the candidate's behest.

Indeed, the regulation contradicts the Santos
letter. The regulation clearly requires that, if a political
action committee pays all costs of writing, designing,
printing, and posting a mass mailing that urges support for a
candidate, the political action committee must be identified
on each piece of mail as the sender. Under the Santos
letter, however, if the political action committee has even
minimal communication' with the candidate about the mailing,

_____________________
3 Regulation 18215(b) provides:

"Made at the behest" means a payment made
under the control or at the direction of,
in cooperation, consultation,
coordination, or concert with, or at the
request or suggestion of a candidate,
(continued...)

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
May 9, 1991
Page 4


the candidate, not the political action committee, will be
identified as the sender. As a result, recipients of the
mailing will receive no notice that the political action
committee had any role in the mailing.

     While we disagree with the interpretation of the
term "sender" in the Santos letter,⁴ our main concerns are
practical and procedural. Practically, we and our clients
are put at peril by the existence of regulations, manuals,

_________________________________________________________________
³(...continued)
     controlled committee, official committee
     of a political party, or organization
     formed or existing primarily for
     political purposes.

⁴For example, if the Republican Party sends out a slate
mailing in support of Republican candidates in the General
Election, it would be ludicrous to require the mailing to
list as senders each of the Republican candidates who had
sufficient communications with their party to cause the
mailing to meet the "at the behest" standard. Similarly, if
a union sent out a slate mailing to its members urging their
support of various candidates it supports, the mailing should
be identified as being "sent" by the union, not by each of
the candidates with whom it has discussed the mailing.

     These examples illustrate a fundamental problem with
the overly broad definition of "at the behest" contained in
Regulation 18215. Webster's Dictionary defines "behest" as:
"an order; bidding; command." Regulation 18215 defines it
instead as including virtually any form of communication:
"cooperation, consultation, coordination" as well as the more
literal "direction" or "control."

     By way of contrast, Webster's Dictionary defines
"behalf" as: "on my side; support, interest, side, e.g." If
the Commission redefined "at the behest" to focus on
something being done at a candidate or committee's bidding or
order, rather than merely being on the candidate's side, the
advice rendered in Santos might make some sense in the
practical world. That still leaves the matter of bringing
the regulation(s) into accord with the desired policy.

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
May 9, 1991
Page 5


and advice letters setting forth conflicting standards.  We
need to have one clear rule.

     Procedurally, we are concerned about the manner in
which the position set forth in the Santos letter was
developed.  Regulation 18435 was adopted in 1976.  It was
last amended in 1983.  In 1985, the Commission staff proposed
an amendment that would have changed the definition of the
term "sender" along the lines of the Santos letter, but the
Commission rejected the amendment.

     In fact, the staff proposed three alternative
versions of amendments to Regulation 18435.  All three were
designed to make specific the requirements for identifying
the sender of a mass mailing.  One alternative would have
expressly stated:

          If a person pays for a portion of [a]
          mass mailing by a nonmonetary
          contribution to a candidate or committee,
          the candidate or committee receiving the
          contribution is a sender of the mailing,
          and the person making the nonmonetary
          contribution is not a sender of the
          mailing.

     The Commission initially approved the alternative.
However, at a subsequent meeting, the Commission rescinded
the adoption of the foregoing alternative and restored the
text of the regulation, unamended, to its prior format.  That
is the same text which is currently in effect and which has
been discussed previously.

     As you know, the Commission, in administering the
provisions of the Political Reform Act, is subject to the
requirements of the Administrative Procedure Act (APA),
Government Code sections 11370 et seq., as it existed
1974.  (See Gov. Code, § 83112; Decision in FPPC v. Office of
Administration Law and Linda Stockdale Brewery, Sacramento
Superior Court No. 512795.)  Rules adopted by an agency "have
no legal effect unless they have been promulgated in
substantial compliance with the APA."  (Armistead v. State
Personnel Board (1978) 22 Cal.3d 198, 204.)  As the Court of
Appeal recently explained,

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
May 9, 1991
Page 6


                    The APA was enacted to establish basic
                minimum procedural requirements for the
                adoption, amendment or repeal of
                administrative regulations promulgated by
                the state's many administrative agencies.
                (Citations omitted.) Its provisions are
                applicable to the exercise of any quasi-
                legislative power conferred by statute.
                (§ 11346.) The APA requires an agency,
                inter alia, to give notice of the
                proposed adoption, amendment or repeal of
                a regulation (§ 11346.7), and to afford
                interested persons the opportunity to
                present comments on the proposed action
                (§ 11346.8). Unless the agency
                promulgates a regulation in substantial
                compliance with the APA, the regulation
                is without legal effect. (Armistead v.
                State Personnel Board (1978) 22 Cal.3d
                198, 204 [.].)

                    (Grier v. Kizer (1990) 219
                    Cal.App.3d 422, 431.)

        The APA requirements must be met, whether the rules
are denominated as policies, interpretations, instructions,
guides, or standards. (See Armistead, supra, 22 Cal.3d at
205.) They apply, whether such rules are included in
internal agency manuals, memoranda, or bulletins, or are
disseminated to the public via circulars, bulletins, or
other publications. (Ibid.)

        The Santos and Bagatelos advice letters, the
Information Manual passage on which they rely, and the recent
Bulletin article do not meet the requirements of the APA. NO
public notice or opportunity for public comment was provided
before they were issued. Yet all ignore the only regulation
on sender identification that this Commission has promulgated
in compliance with the APA -- a regulation the Commission
decided not to amend when the staff proposed doing so via
proper APA procedures. Thus, Santos and the others are
unenforceable. The passage in the Information Manual in

_____________________
        5 As noted earlier, similar "advice" was given in the
June, 1990 FPPC Bulletin.

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
May 9, 1991
Page 7


particular, because it purports to establish a rule governing
all candidates and committees with disclosure duties under
section 84305 of the Act, is without legal effect.6

Accordingly, the California Political Attorneys
Association respectfully requests that the advice given in
the Santos and Bagatelοs advice letters be reconsidered and
withdrawn. We also urge prompt action to delete from the
1991 Information Manual, which is due to be released soon,
the passage on which those advice letters rely.

The staff or members of the Commission may then
wish to reopen the question of amending Regulation 18435. If
so, the California Political Attorneys Association will
submit comments on the merits of the proposed changes.




6 In 1982, the Administrative Procedure Act was amended
to give the Office of Administrative Law (OAL) power to issue
"determinations" that guides, bulletins and manuals are, in
fact, "regulations" subject to APA procedural requirements.
(See Gov. Code, § 11347.5(b).) A superior Court recently
ruled that the OAL may not issue such determinations as to
this Commission's guidelines. (FPPC v. Office of
Administrative Law and Linda Stockdale Brewer, supra,
Sacramento Superior Court No. 512795.) That ruling affects
only the scope of the OAL's power and does not serve to
validate the Santos and Bagatelοs advice letters or the
Information Manual passage on which they are based. The
Supreme Court decided Armistead v. State Personnel Board in
1978, based on the APA as it existed in 1974. Armistead's
holding, that rules adopted by an agency "have no legal
effect unless they have been promulgated in substantial
compliance with the APA" (22 Cal.3d at 204), continues to
apply with full force to the actions of this Commission.

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
May 9, 1991
Page 8


Thank you for considering our suggestions. I would
be happy to discuss this letter with members of the staff at
any time.

Sincerely,

[signature]

Lowell Finley
Member, Board of Directors
California Political Attorneys
Association

LF:bk

sections.question_synthetic: The California Political Attorneys Association requests reconsideration of the Santos Advice Letter (I-90-650), which concluded that candidates are deemed senders of mass mailings sent at their behest, arguing that this interpretation contradicts FPPC Regulation 18435 and lacks proper APA compliance.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request/correspondence to the FPPC, not an FPPC response letter. The document is a formal request for reconsideration of a prior advice letter. No standard question/conclusion sections exist; instead, the letter presents detailed legal arguments challenging a prior FPPC position. Document cites Regulation 18435 and argues procedural deficiencies in how Santos advice letter was developed.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: The California Political Attorneys Association requests reconsideration of the Santos Advice Letter (I-90-650), which concluded that candidates are deemed senders of mass mailings sent at their behest, arguing that this interpretation contradicts FPPC Regulation 18435 and lacks proper APA compliance.

embedding.summary: A law firm representing the California Political Attorneys Association requests that the FPPC reconsider and withdraw the Santos Advice Letter regarding mass mailing sender identification, arguing that the letter's interpretation conflicts with Regulation 18435, lacks procedural compliance with the Administrative Procedure Act, and creates confusion among practitioners.
